% The only predicate needed to represent the matching of borrower, due_date, and title.
% Each book is uniquely identified by its borrower, due_date, and title.

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{B1=B2; D1=D2; T1=T2}=0 :- match(B1,D1,T1), match(B2,D2,T2), (B1,D1,T1)!=(B2,D2,T2).

% 1. The four books are Rosa's book, the book due on September 22, Dancing Well and the book due on September 15.
% These are the four books, so they must be represented in the match predicate.
% No additional constraint needed — they are covered by the domain.

% 2. Heaven's Seal is either Vicki's book or the book due on September 8.
{B="Vicki"; D=8}=1 :- match(B,D,T), T="Heaven's Seal".

% 3.1 Heaven's Seal and the title due on September 22 are different.
% So, the book "Heaven's Seal" is not the same as the book due on 22.
% That is, if T="Heaven's Seal", then D!=22.
:- match(B,D,T), T="Heaven's Seal", D=22.

% 3.2 Heaven's Seal was taken out by Cory or taken out by Vicki.
{B="Cory"; B="Vicki"}=1 :- match(B,D,T), T="Heaven's Seal".

% 3.3 The title due on September 22 was taken out by Cory or taken out by Vicki.
{B="Cory"; B="Vicki"}=1 :- match(B,D,T), D=22.

% 4. Sherrie's book was due 2 weeks before Stars Below.
% Due dates: 1, 8, 15, 22 → differences of 14: (1,15), (8,22)
% So, if Sherrie's book is due on D, then "Stars Below" is due on D+14.
% So, D+14 must be in {15,22} → D in {1,8}
% So, D must be 1 or 8.
% And if D=1, then T="Stars Below" must be due on 15.
% If D=8, then T="Stars Below" must be due on 22.
% So: match("Sherrie", D1, T1), match(B, D2, "Stars Below"), D2 = D1 + 14.
% We can encode this as:
D2 = D1 + 14 :- match("Sherrie", D1, _), match(_, D2, "Stars Below").
